answer: The recommended inspection interval for the HPC is every 30 operational cycles under nominal conditions, reduced to every 10 cycles once any HPC parameter crosses its alarm threshold.
sources_text:
[doc_id=manual-hpc | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Borescope inspection of the HPC stages is recommended every **30 operational cycles**
under nominal conditions, reduced to every **10 cycles** once any HPC parameter
crosses its alarm threshold.

[doc_id=manual-hpt | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[High-Pressure Turbine (HPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
HPT clearance and seal inspection every **30 operational cycles**, aligned with the
HPC schedule since the two are mechanically coupled on the high-pressure shaft.

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Fan blade and containment inspection every **50 operational cycles**. Because the fan
is not the primary fault mode in this fleet, routine intervals are longer than for the
HPC.

[doc_id=manual-lpc | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Combined gas-path inspection every **40 operational cycles**; the LPC is typically
inspected alongside the HPC because the two share the core gas path.

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm the primary signature on at least the last 5 operational cycles (suppress
   single-cycle noise spikes).
2. Reduce HPC borescope inspection interval to every 10 cycles.
3. Perform a compressor wash; re-baseline T30 and Ps30 after the wash.
4. If T30 and Ps30 remain in alarm after wash, schedule HPC module removal at the next
   maintenance opportunity and begin RUL tracking for end-of-life planning.
5. Check whether T50 (LPT) has also entered alarm — co-occurrence indicates
   degradation has propagated and shortens the remaining-useful-life estimate.

answer: Fault code FC-HPC-001 indicates High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) degradation. Its primary sensor signature requires T30 to be rising and ≥ 1607.89 degR, Ps30 to be rising and ≥ 48.10 psia, P30 to be falling and ≤ 551.16 psia, and phi to be falling and ≤ 519.69 pps/psi.
sources_text:
[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: Usage notes]
[Fault Code Reference Index — Usage notes]
- **HPC degradation (FC-HPC-001) is the dominant fault mode in this fleet.** When in
  doubt, evaluate the HPC signature first.
- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.
- Codes are not mutually exclusive: late-life engines frequently show FC-HPC-001 and
  FC-LPT-001 together as degradation propagates downstream.

[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: overview]
[Fault Code Reference Index — overview]
This index maps fault codes to their triggering subsystem, the primary sensor
signature, and the detailed procedure document. Raise a fault code when the listed
primary parameters are simultaneously in alarm.

| Code | Subsystem | Primary signature | Procedure |
|------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| FC-HPC-001 | HPC | T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR and Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia, with falling P30 / phi | see `fault-FC-HPC-001` |
| FC-LPT-001 | LPT | T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR with W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s | see `fault-FC-LPT-001` |
| FC-FAN-001 | Fan | BPR above nominal with no corresponding HPC alarm | manual-fan, §4 |
| FC-HPT-001 | HPT | W31 below nominal coolant bleed | manual-hpt, §4 |

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Primary signature (all should be present to raise the code)]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Primary signature (all should be present to raise the code)]
| Sensor | Symbol | Direction | Alarm condition |
|--------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| 3  | T30  | rising  | ≥ 1607.89 degR |
| 11 | Ps30 | rising  | ≥ 48.10 psia   |
| 7  | P30  | falling | ≤ 551.16 psia  |
| 12 | phi  | falling | ≤ 519.69 pps/psi |

[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: overview]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — overview]
**Engine:** 47 **Date:** 2026-03-18 **Raised by:** prognostics review
**Fault code:** FC-HPC-001 (HPC degradation)

[doc_id=fault-FC-LPT-001 | section: Primary signature]
[FC-LPT-001 — Low-Pressure Turbine Thermal Degradation — Primary signature]
| Sensor | Symbol | Direction | Alarm condition |
|--------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| 4  | T50 | rising  | ≥ 1428.11 degR |
| 21 | W32 | falling | ≤ 23.00 lbm/s  |

answer: The alarm threshold for the LPT outlet temperature T50 is 1428.11 degR.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1003-engine76 | section: Observation]
[WO-1003 — Engine 76 LPT exhaust over-temperature — Observation]
Engine 76's LPT outlet temperature (T50) reached 1430.6 degR, above the **1428.11
degR** alarm threshold, sustained over 7 cycles. Coolant bleed (W32) was trending
toward its low threshold. HPC parameters were nominal, so this was treated as a
primary LPT event rather than propagated HPC degradation.

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR:** LPT over-temperature. Inspect for blade distress and
  exhaust-path blockage. Persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in
  engines whose primary degradation has propagated downstream.
- **W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s:** falling LPT coolant bleed; inspect the cooling circuit and
  cross-check W31 (sensor 20) for a shared supply fault. See fault code FC-LPT-001.

[doc_id=wo-1009-engine91 | section: Observation]
[WO-1009 — Engine 91 combined LPT thermal + coolant event — Observation]
Engine 91 presented the full FC-LPT-001 signature: LPT outlet temperature (T50) at
1431.2 degR, above the **1428.11 degR** threshold, with LPT coolant bleed (W32) at
22.9 lbm/s, below the **23.00 lbm/s** low threshold. HPC parameters were also
elevated, indicating degradation had propagated downstream from the core gas path.

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 2. Monitored parameters]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 2. Monitored parameters]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 4  | T50 | LPT outlet total temperature (degR) | 1390.08 – 1415.43 | ≥ 1428.11 (high) |
| 21 | W32 | LPT coolant bleed (lbm/s)           | 23.18 – 23.53     | ≤ 23.00 (low)    |

[doc_id=wo-1003-engine76 | section: Status]
[WO-1003 — Engine 76 LPT exhaust over-temperature — Status]
Open — monitor T50; co-occurrence with an HPC alarm would shorten the RUL estimate.

answer: For engine 24's HPC over-temperature advisory, the action taken included reducing the HPC borescope interval to every 10 cycles per FC-HPC-001, scheduling a compressor wash, and beginning RUL tracking. The borescope showed early-stage blade fouling consistent with efficiency loss.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — Action taken]
Reduced HPC borescope interval to every 10 cycles per FC-HPC-001. Compressor wash
scheduled. Began RUL tracking. Borescope showed early-stage blade fouling consistent
with efficiency loss.

[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: overview]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — overview]
**Engine:** 24 **Date:** 2026-03-04 **Raised by:** line monitoring
**Fault code:** FC-HPC-001 (HPC degradation)

[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: Status]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — Status]
Open — re-baseline T30/Ps30 after wash; escalate to module removal if still in alarm.

[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — Action taken]
Reviewed HPC parameters — all nominal, so this was logged as an isolated fan advisory,
not FC-HPC-001. No hardware action; flagged for re-review in 25 cycles.

[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: Observation]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — Observation]
Trend review flagged Engine 24's HPC discharge temperature (T30) climbing across the
last 12 cycles, reaching 1609.4 degR — above the **1607.89 degR** alarm threshold.
Static discharge pressure (Ps30) reached 48.2 psia, also above its **48.10 psia**
threshold. Total discharge pressure (P30) and the fuel-flow ratio (phi) were trending
down, consistent with the FC-HPC-001 signature.

answer: I don't have enough information to answer that.
The alarm condition for the HPT coolant bleed W31 is when it is less than or equal to 38.35 lbm/s.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1008-engine05 | section: Observation]
[WO-1008 — Engine 5 HPT coolant-bleed decline — Observation]
Engine 5's HPT coolant bleed (W31) declined to 38.30 lbm/s, below the **38.35 lbm/s**
low alarm threshold and under the **38.64** nominal minimum. LPT coolant bleed (W32)
remained nominal, so this was treated as an HPT-local cooling issue, not a shared
supply fault.

[doc_id=manual-hpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[High-Pressure Turbine (HPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **W31 ≤ 38.35 lbm/s:** declining HPT coolant bleed. Inspect coolant passages and
  high-pressure shaft seals. A simultaneous decline in LPT coolant bleed (W32, sensor
  21) points to a shared cooling-supply problem rather than an isolated HPT fault.

[doc_id=wo-1008-engine05 | section: Status]
[WO-1008 — Engine 5 HPT coolant-bleed decline — Status]
Open — HPT seal service pending; monitor W31 and watch W32 for any shared-supply signature.

[doc_id=manual-hpt | section: 2. Monitored parameters]
[High-Pressure Turbine (HPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 2. Monitored parameters]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 20 | W31 | HPT coolant bleed (lbm/s) | 38.64 – 39.22 | ≤ 38.35 (low) |

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR:** LPT over-temperature. Inspect for blade distress and
  exhaust-path blockage. Persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in
  engines whose primary degradation has propagated downstream.
- **W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s:** falling LPT coolant bleed; inspect the cooling circuit and
  cross-check W31 (sensor 20) for a shared supply fault. See fault code FC-LPT-001.

answer: The Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) should be inspected every 40 operational cycles.
sources_text:
[doc_id=manual-lpc | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Combined gas-path inspection every **40 operational cycles**; the LPC is typically
inspected alongside the HPC because the two share the core gas path.

[doc_id=manual-lpc | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T24 ≥ 644.15 degR:** rising LPC discharge temperature. On its own this is usually
  mild; if it accompanies an HPC alarm (see FC-HPC-001) treat it as confirmation of
  core gas-path degradation rather than an isolated LPC fault.

[doc_id=manual-lpc | section: 1. Overview]
[Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 1. Overview]
The low-pressure compressor (booster) raises airflow pressure between the fan and the
HPC. Its health is tracked primarily through the LPC outlet total temperature, which
rises as booster efficiency degrades.

[doc_id=manual-lpc | section: 2. Monitored parameters]
[Low-Pressure Compressor (LPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 2. Monitored parameters]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 2 | T24 | LPC outlet total temperature (degR) | 641.54 – 643.28 | ≥ 644.15 (high) |

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
LPT gas-path and exhaust inspection every **35 operational cycles**.

answer: The fault codes and their corresponding subsystems are: FC-HPC-001 for the HPC subsystem, FC-LPT-001 for the LPT subsystem, FC-FAN-001 for the Fan subsystem, and FC-HPT-001 for the HPT subsystem.

signature, and the detailed procedure document. Raise a fault code when the listed
primary parameters are simultaneously in alarm.

| FC-HPC-001 | HPC | T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR and Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia, with falling P30 / phi | see `fault-FC-HPC-001` |

  doubt, evaluate the HPC signature first.
- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.
- Codes are not mutually exclusive: late-life engines frequently show FC-HPC-001 and
  FC-LPT-001 together as degradation propagates downstream.

- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.

This index maps fault codes to their triggering subsystem, the primary sensor
signature, and the detailed procedure document. Raise a fault code when the listed
primary parameters are simultaneously in alarm.

| Code | Subsystem | Primary signature | Procedure |

| FC-HPC-001 | HPC | T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR and Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia, with falling P30 / phi | see `fault-FC-HPC-001` |

[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR:** LPT over-temperature. Inspect for blade distress and
  exhaust-path blockage. Persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in
  engines whose primary degradation has propagated downstream.

answer: The alarm thresholds for core speed are Nc ≥ 9092.00 rpm and NRc ≥ 8169.77 rpm.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1004-engine12 | section: Observation]
[WO-1004 — Engine 12 core overspeed advisory — Observation]
Engine 12's physical core speed (Nc) reached 9093.5 rpm, just above the **9092.00 rpm**
alarm threshold, with corrected core speed (NRc) at 8171.0 rpm, above its **8169.77
rpm** threshold. Rising core speed is a system-level symptom: the core is compensating
for gas-path efficiency loss.

[doc_id=manual-core-engine | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Core Shaft & Engine-Level Monitoring Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **Nc ≥ 9092.00 rpm / NRc ≥ 8169.77 rpm:** rising core speed indicates the core is
  working harder to hold thrust — a system-level symptom of gas-path degradation.
  Localize using HPC parameters (FC-HPC-001).
- **htBleed ≥ 397.50:** rising bleed enthalpy reflects increasing core temperatures
  overall; a strong end-of-life indicator when combined with rising T30 and T50.

[doc_id=manual-core-engine | section: 2. Monitored parameters]
[Core Shaft & Engine-Level Monitoring Manual — 2. Monitored parameters]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 9  | Nc      | Physical core speed (rpm)   | 9038.00 – 9074.00 | ≥ 9092.00 (high) |
| 14 | NRc     | Corrected core speed (rpm)  | 8120.73 – 8153.42 | ≥ 8169.77 (high) |
| 17 | htBleed | Bleed enthalpy (index)      | 390.00 – 395.00   | ≥ 397.50 (high)  |
| 10 | epr     | Engine pressure ratio (–)   | constant ≈ 1.30   | non-informative  |

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Secondary / confirming indicators]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Secondary / confirming indicators]
- Core speed Nc rising toward **9092.00 rpm** as the core compensates for lost efficiency.
- Bleed enthalpy htBleed rising toward **397.50** as overall core temperatures climb.

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **BPR ≥ 8.54:** rising bypass ratio indicates the core is passing less of the
  total flow — often a secondary symptom of core/HPC degradation rather than a fan
  fault. Correlate with HPC parameters before attributing to the fan.
- **Nf ≥ 2388.30 rpm / NRf ≥ 2388.28 rpm:** physical and corrected fan speed creeping
  above nominal. Confirm against demanded fan speed; a gap suggests control-loop drift.

answer: Once FC-HPC-001 is confirmed, the recommended procedure is to: 1) Confirm the primary signature on at least the last 5 operational cycles. 2) Reduce HPC borescope inspection interval to every 10 cycles. 3) Perform a compressor wash and re-baseline T30 and Ps30 after the wash. 4) If T30 and Ps30 remain in alarm after the wash, schedule HPC module removal at the next maintenance opportunity and begin RUL tracking for end-of-life planning. 5) Check whether T50 (LPT) has also entered alarm, as co-occurrence indicates degradation has propagated and shortens the remaining-useful-life estimate.
sources_text:
[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: Usage notes]
[Fault Code Reference Index — Usage notes]
- **HPC degradation (FC-HPC-001) is the dominant fault mode in this fleet.** When in
  doubt, evaluate the HPC signature first.
- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.
- Codes are not mutually exclusive: late-life engines frequently show FC-HPC-001 and
  FC-LPT-001 together as degradation propagates downstream.

[doc_id=fault-FC-LPT-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-LPT-001 — Low-Pressure Turbine Thermal Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm sustained T50 rise over at least 5 cycles, with W32 trending down.
2. Inspect LPT blades and exhaust path for distress and blockage.
3. Inspect the LPT coolant circuit; if W31 (HPT coolant, sensor 20) is also low,
   treat as a shared cooling-supply fault rather than an isolated LPT problem.
4. When FC-LPT-001 co-occurs with FC-HPC-001, prioritize end-of-life planning — the
   combination is a strong late-life indicator.

[doc_id=wo-1007-engine58 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1007 — Engine 58 rising bleed enthalpy — Action taken]
Raised FC-HPC-001. Compressor wash performed; began close RUL tracking given the
combined late-life signature. Recommended end-of-life planning review.

[doc_id=manual-hpc | section: 4. Alarm response]
[High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR:** HPC discharge over-temperature. Inspect for blade fouling,
  tip-clearance loss, and seal wear. Correlate with Ps30 and phi.
- **Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia** with **P30 ≤ 551.16 psia:** classic HPC efficiency loss
  signature — the stage is generating more static rise but less recoverable total
  pressure. Schedule wash and clearance check.
- **phi ≤ 519.69 pps/psi:** fuel-flow-to-pressure ratio falling; cross-check fuel
  metering and confirm against T30 trend before acting.

When two or more HPC parameters are simultaneously in alarm, raise fault code
**FC-HPC-001** (HPC degradation) and follow that procedure.

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm the primary signature on at least the last 5 operational cycles (suppress
   single-cycle noise spikes).
2. Reduce HPC borescope inspection interval to every 10 cycles.
3. Perform a compressor wash; re-baseline T30 and Ps30 after the wash.
4. If T30 and Ps30 remain in alarm after wash, schedule HPC module removal at the next
   maintenance opportunity and begin RUL tracking for end-of-life planning.
5. Check whether T50 (LPT) has also entered alarm — co-occurrence indicates
   degradation has propagated and shortens the remaining-useful-life estimate.

answer: The work order for engine 58 found rising bleed enthalpy, with T30 and htBleed both in alarm. The fault code raised was FC-HPC-001.

answer: The non-informative fan sensors under FD001 conditions are: fan inlet temperature (T2, sensor 1), fan inlet pressure (P2, sensor 5), bypass-duct pressure (P15, sensor 6), demanded fan speed (Nf_dmd, sensor 18), and demanded corrected fan speed (PCNfR_dmd, sensor 19).
sources_text:
[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 1. Overview]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 1. Overview]
The fan provides bypass thrust and feeds the core. Under the single sea-level
operating condition of this fleet (FD001), several fan-related channels are held
effectively constant by the control system and carry **no degradation signal**:
fan inlet temperature (T2, sensor 1), fan inlet pressure (P2, sensor 5), bypass-duct
pressure (P15, sensor 6), demanded fan speed (Nf_dmd, sensor 18) and demanded
corrected fan speed (PCNfR_dmd, sensor 19). These are recorded for completeness but
are **excluded from trend monitoring**.

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 2. Monitored parameters (informative)]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 2. Monitored parameters (informative)]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 8  | Nf  | Physical fan speed (rpm)   | 2387.94 – 2388.18 | ≥ 2388.30 (high) |
| 13 | NRf | Corrected fan speed (rpm)  | 2387.94 – 2388.17 | ≥ 2388.28 (high) |
| 15 | BPR | Bypass ratio (–)           | 8.36 – 8.48       | ≥ 8.54 (high)    |

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 3. Inspection interval]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 3. Inspection interval]
Fan blade and containment inspection every **50 operational cycles**. Because the fan
is not the primary fault mode in this fleet, routine intervals are longer than for the
HPC.

[doc_id=manual-combustor | section: 2. Monitored parameters]
[Combustor Operation & Maintenance Manual — 2. Monitored parameters]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Behaviour |
|--------|--------|----------|-----------|
| 16 | farB | Burner fuel-air ratio (–) | Constant ≈ 0.03; non-informative for trending |

[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: overview]
[Fault Code Reference Index — overview]
This index maps fault codes to their triggering subsystem, the primary sensor
signature, and the detailed procedure document. Raise a fault code when the listed
primary parameters are simultaneously in alarm.

| Code | Subsystem | Primary signature | Procedure |
|------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| FC-HPC-001 | HPC | T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR and Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia, with falling P30 / phi | see `fault-FC-HPC-001` |
| FC-LPT-001 | LPT | T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR with W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s | see `fault-FC-LPT-001` |
| FC-FAN-001 | Fan | BPR above nominal with no corresponding HPC alarm | manual-fan, §4 |
| FC-HPT-001 | HPT | W31 below nominal coolant bleed | manual-hpt, §4 |

answer: Engine 47 is showing a known fault pattern associated with HPC degradation, as indicated by the fault code FC-HPC-001. The manual suggests evaluating the HPC signature first when in doubt. The observation notes that Engine 47's Ps30 readings are rising, but the P30 and fuel-flow ratio are below their respective thresholds, consistent with the 'pressure-loss-first' presentation of HPC degradation. The manual does not provide specific corrective actions for elevated Ps30 readings alone, but the fault code reference suggests focusing on the HPC signature.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: Observation]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — Observation]
Engine 47 showed HPC total discharge pressure (P30) falling to 550.8 psia, below the
**551.16 psia** low alarm threshold, with the fuel-flow ratio (phi) at 519.4 pps/psi,
under its **519.69** low threshold. T30 was elevated but not yet in alarm. This is the
"pressure-loss-first" presentation of HPC degradation.

[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: overview]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — overview]
**Engine:** 47 **Date:** 2026-03-18 **Raised by:** prognostics review
**Fault code:** FC-HPC-001 (HPC degradation)

[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: Observation]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — Observation]
Trend review flagged Engine 24's HPC discharge temperature (T30) climbing across the
last 12 cycles, reaching 1609.4 degR — above the **1607.89 degR** alarm threshold.
Static discharge pressure (Ps30) reached 48.2 psia, also above its **48.10 psia**
threshold. Total discharge pressure (P30) and the fuel-flow ratio (phi) were trending
down, consistent with the FC-HPC-001 signature.

[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: Status]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — Status]
Open — re-baseline T30/Ps30 after wash; escalate to module removal if still in alarm.

[doc_id=fault-code-reference | section: Usage notes]
[Fault Code Reference Index — Usage notes]
- **HPC degradation (FC-HPC-001) is the dominant fault mode in this fleet.** When in
  doubt, evaluate the HPC signature first.
- A single parameter in alarm is an *advisory*, not a fault. Raise a coded fault only
  when the full primary signature is present, to suppress sensor-noise false alarms.
- Codes are not mutually exclusive: late-life engines frequently show FC-HPC-001 and
  FC-LPT-001 together as degradation propagates downstream.

[TS1 | telemetry:engine47/sensor11/trend(last_n=50)]
Engine 47 Ps30 (sensor 11) over last 50 cycles: 47.55 → 48.28 (rising).

answer: Engine 12 is experiencing a rising core speed, which is a known issue. The core speed (Nc) reached 9093.5 rpm, exceeding the alarm threshold of 9092.00 rpm. This is a system-level symptom indicating the core is compensating for gas-path efficiency loss, with the High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) identified as the root cause. The issue is being tracked under fault code FC-HPC-001.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1004-engine12 | section: Observation]
[WO-1004 — Engine 12 core overspeed advisory — Observation]
Engine 12's physical core speed (Nc) reached 9093.5 rpm, just above the **9092.00 rpm**
alarm threshold, with corrected core speed (NRc) at 8171.0 rpm, above its **8169.77
rpm** threshold. Rising core speed is a system-level symptom: the core is compensating
for gas-path efficiency loss.

[doc_id=wo-1004-engine12 | section: Status]
[WO-1004 — Engine 12 core overspeed advisory — Status]
Open — core speed is the symptom; HPC is the root cause. Tracking under FC-HPC-001.

[doc_id=manual-core-engine | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Core Shaft & Engine-Level Monitoring Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **Nc ≥ 9092.00 rpm / NRc ≥ 8169.77 rpm:** rising core speed indicates the core is
  working harder to hold thrust — a system-level symptom of gas-path degradation.
  Localize using HPC parameters (FC-HPC-001).
- **htBleed ≥ 397.50:** rising bleed enthalpy reflects increasing core temperatures
  overall; a strong end-of-life indicator when combined with rising T30 and T50.

[doc_id=fault-FC-HPC-001 | section: Secondary / confirming indicators]
[FC-HPC-001 — High-Pressure Compressor Degradation — Secondary / confirming indicators]
- Core speed Nc rising toward **9092.00 rpm** as the core compensates for lost efficiency.
- Bleed enthalpy htBleed rising toward **397.50** as overall core temperatures climb.

answer: Engine 5's HPT coolant bleed W31 is in alarm as it has declined to 38.30 lbm/s, which is below the low alarm threshold of 38.35 lbm/s. The manual advises inspecting coolant passages and high-pressure shaft seals when W31 is at or below 38.35 lbm/s.
sources_text:

[WO-1008 — Engine 5 HPT coolant-bleed decline — Observation]
Engine 5's HPT coolant bleed (W31) declined to 38.30 lbm/s, below the **38.35 lbm/s**
low alarm threshold and under the **38.64** nominal minimum. LPT coolant bleed (W32)
remained nominal, so this was treated as an HPT-local cooling issue, not a shared
supply fault.

[doc_id=wo-1008-engine05 | section: Status]

[doc_id=manual-hpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[High-Pressure Turbine (HPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **W31 ≤ 38.35 lbm/s:** declining HPT coolant bleed. Inspect coolant passages and
  high-pressure shaft seals. A simultaneous decline in LPT coolant bleed (W32, sensor
  21) points to a shared cooling-supply problem rather than an isolated HPT fault.

[doc_id=manual-hpt | section: 2. Monitored parameters]

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR:** LPT over-temperature. Inspect for blade distress and
  exhaust-path blockage. Persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in
  engines whose primary degradation has propagated downstream.
- **W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s:** falling LPT coolant bleed; inspect the cooling circuit and
  cross-check W31 (sensor 20) for a shared supply fault. See fault code FC-LPT-001.

answer: Yes, the dropping HPC discharge pressure P30 in Engine 47 is a known fault. It is associated with fault code FC-HPC-001, indicating HPC degradation. The observed P30 of 550.8 psia is below the 551.16 psia low alarm threshold, confirming the fault signature.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: Observation]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — Observation]
Engine 47 showed HPC total discharge pressure (P30) falling to 550.8 psia, below the
**551.16 psia** low alarm threshold, with the fuel-flow ratio (phi) at 519.4 pps/psi,
under its **519.69** low threshold. T30 was elevated but not yet in alarm. This is the
"pressure-loss-first" presentation of HPC degradation.

[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: overview]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — overview]
**Engine:** 47 **Date:** 2026-03-18 **Raised by:** prognostics review
**Fault code:** FC-HPC-001 (HPC degradation)

[doc_id=wo-1001-engine24 | section: Observation]
[WO-1001 — Engine 24 HPC over-temperature advisory — Observation]
Trend review flagged Engine 24's HPC discharge temperature (T30) climbing across the
last 12 cycles, reaching 1609.4 degR — above the **1607.89 degR** alarm threshold.
Static discharge pressure (Ps30) reached 48.2 psia, also above its **48.10 psia**
threshold. Total discharge pressure (P30) and the fuel-flow ratio (phi) were trending
down, consistent with the FC-HPC-001 signature.

[doc_id=manual-hpc | section: 4. Alarm response]
[High-Pressure Compressor (HPC) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T30 ≥ 1607.89 degR:** HPC discharge over-temperature. Inspect for blade fouling,
  tip-clearance loss, and seal wear. Correlate with Ps30 and phi.
- **Ps30 ≥ 48.10 psia** with **P30 ≤ 551.16 psia:** classic HPC efficiency loss
  signature — the stage is generating more static rise but less recoverable total
  pressure. Schedule wash and clearance check.
- **phi ≤ 519.69 pps/psi:** fuel-flow-to-pressure ratio falling; cross-check fuel
  metering and confirm against T30 trend before acting.

When two or more HPC parameters are simultaneously in alarm, raise fault code
**FC-HPC-001** (HPC degradation) and follow that procedure.

[doc_id=wo-1002-engine47 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1002 — Engine 47 HPC pressure-loss investigation — Action taken]
Confirmed signature over 6 consecutive cycles to rule out sensor noise. Performed
compressor wash; P30 recovered partially to 552.0 psia. Continued monitoring.

[TS1 | telemetry:engine47/sensor7/status@cycle214]
Engine 47 P30 at cycle 214: 551.7 (nominal; nominal 552.51–555.21).

This indicates that the core is working harder to maintain thrust due to gas-path efficiency loss. The issue is known and is being tracked under FC-HPC-001, with the high-pressure compressor (HPC) identified as the root cause.

rpm** threshold. Rising core speed is a system-level symptom: the core is compensating
for gas-path efficiency loss.

- **Nc ≥ 9092.00 rpm / NRc ≥ 8169.77 rpm:** rising core speed indicates the core is
  working harder to hold thrust — a system-level symptom of gas-path degradation.
  Localize using HPC parameters (FC-HPC-001).
- **htBleed ≥ 397.50:** rising bleed enthalpy reflects increasing core temperatures
  overall; a strong end-of-life indicator when combined with rising T30 and T50.

[doc_id=manual-core-engine | section: 1. Overview]
[Core Shaft & Engine-Level Monitoring Manual — 1. Overview]
This manual covers shaft speeds and engine-level indicators that span subsystems. Core
speed rises as the engine compensates for gas-path efficiency loss; bleed enthalpy
rises with overall thermal degradation. Engine pressure ratio (epr, sensor 10) is held
constant at **1.30** under FD001 conditions and is non-informative for trending.

answer: The elevated bypass ratio (BPR) for Engine 33 is likely not a fan fault. The BPR drifted to 8.55, which is above the alarm threshold of 8.54. According to the fan manual, a rising BPR without a corresponding HPC alarm is usually a secondary effect rather than a fan fault. The HPC parameters were reviewed and found to be nominal, leading to the issue being logged as an isolated fan advisory, not an HPC fault.
sources_text:
[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: Observation]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — Observation]
Engine 33's bypass ratio (BPR) drifted to 8.55, just over the **8.54** alarm threshold
and above the **8.48** nominal maximum. Per the fan manual, rising BPR with no
corresponding HPC alarm is usually a secondary effect rather than a fan fault.

[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: overview]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — overview]
**Engine:** 33 **Date:** 2026-05-01 **Raised by:** trend review
**Fault code:** FC-FAN-001 (advisory)

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **BPR ≥ 8.54:** rising bypass ratio indicates the core is passing less of the
  total flow — often a secondary symptom of core/HPC degradation rather than a fan
  fault. Correlate with HPC parameters before attributing to the fan.
- **Nf ≥ 2388.30 rpm / NRf ≥ 2388.28 rpm:** physical and corrected fan speed creeping
  above nominal. Confirm against demanded fan speed; a gap suggests control-loop drift.

[doc_id=wo-1006-engine33 | section: Action taken]
[WO-1006 — Engine 33 bypass-ratio drift — Action taken]
Reviewed HPC parameters — all nominal, so this was logged as an isolated fan advisory,
not FC-HPC-001. No hardware action; flagged for re-review in 25 cycles.

[doc_id=manual-fan | section: 2. Monitored parameters (informative)]
[Fan Module Operation & Maintenance Manual — 2. Monitored parameters (informative)]
| Sensor | Symbol | Quantity | Nominal range | Alarm |
|--------|--------|----------|---------------|-------|
| 8  | Nf  | Physical fan speed (rpm)   | 2387.94 – 2388.18 | ≥ 2388.30 (high) |
| 13 | NRf | Corrected fan speed (rpm)  | 2387.94 – 2388.17 | ≥ 2388.28 (high) |
| 15 | BPR | Bypass ratio (–)           | 8.36 – 8.48       | ≥ 8.54 (high)    |

If Ps30 is above 48.10 psia and P30 is below 551.16 psia, it suggests HPC efficiency loss, requiring a wash and clearance check. If the issue persists after these actions, escalate to module removal.

[doc_id=wo-1008-engine05 | section: Observation]
[WO-1008 — Engine 5 HPT coolant-bleed decline — Observation]
Engine 5's HPT coolant bleed (W31) declined to 38.30 lbm/s, below the **38.35 lbm/s**
low alarm threshold and under the **38.64** nominal minimum. LPT coolant bleed (W32)
remained nominal, so this was treated as an HPT-local cooling issue, not a shared
supply fault.

[doc_id=wo-1009-engine91 | section: Observation]
[WO-1009 — Engine 91 combined LPT thermal + coolant event — Observation]
Engine 91 presented the full FC-LPT-001 signature: LPT outlet temperature (T50) at
1431.2 degR, above the **1428.11 degR** threshold, with LPT coolant bleed (W32) at
22.9 lbm/s, below the **23.00 lbm/s** low threshold. HPC parameters were also
elevated, indicating degradation had propagated downstream from the core gas path.

[doc_id=wo-1008-engine05 | section: Status]
[WO-1008 — Engine 5 HPT coolant-bleed decline — Status]
Open — HPT seal service pending; monitor W31 and watch W32 for any shared-supply signature.

[doc_id=wo-1003-engine76 | section: Observation]
[WO-1003 — Engine 76 LPT exhaust over-temperature — Observation]
Engine 76's LPT outlet temperature (T50) reached 1430.6 degR, above the **1428.11
degR** alarm threshold, sustained over 7 cycles. Coolant bleed (W32) was trending
toward its low threshold. HPC parameters were nominal, so this was treated as a
primary LPT event rather than propagated HPC degradation.

[doc_id=wo-1009-engine91 | section: Observation]
[WO-1009 — Engine 91 combined LPT thermal + coolant event — Observation]
Engine 91 presented the full FC-LPT-001 signature: LPT outlet temperature (T50) at
1431.2 degR, above the **1428.11 degR** threshold, with LPT coolant bleed (W32) at
22.9 lbm/s, below the **23.00 lbm/s** low threshold. HPC parameters were also
elevated, indicating degradation had propagated downstream from the core gas path.

[doc_id=manual-lpt | section: 4. Alarm response]
[Low-Pressure Turbine (LPT) Operation & Maintenance Manual — 4. Alarm response]
- **T50 ≥ 1428.11 degR:** LPT over-temperature. Inspect for blade distress and
  exhaust-path blockage. Persistent high T50 is a leading indicator of end-of-life in
  engines whose primary degradation has propagated downstream.
- **W32 ≤ 23.00 lbm/s:** falling LPT coolant bleed; inspect the cooling circuit and
  cross-check W31 (sensor 20) for a shared supply fault. See fault code FC-LPT-001.

[doc_id=fault-FC-LPT-001 | section: Procedure]
[FC-LPT-001 — Low-Pressure Turbine Thermal Degradation — Procedure]
1. Confirm sustained T50 rise over at least 5 cycles, with W32 trending down.
2. Inspect LPT blades and exhaust path for distress and blockage.
3. Inspect the LPT coolant circuit; if W31 (HPT coolant, sensor 20) is also low,
   treat as a shared cooling-supply fault rather than an isolated LPT problem.
4. When FC-LPT-001 co-occurs with FC-HPC-001, prioritize end-of-life planning — the
   combination is a strong late-life indicator.
